content.text: Escalation Chains: Why Structure Matters Under Pressure

An escalation chain is a pre-committed set of decisions about who gets involved in an incident, in what order, and after how much time. Its value is not that it tells engineers something they do not know — it is that it removes the need to make those decisions in real time while managing an incident. An on-call engineer who is simultaneously diagnosing a P1 and trying to decide whether to wake up the VP of Engineering will delay both tasks. The escalation chain makes the notification decision automatic.

GenAI services have escalation paths that differ from standard software services in two ways. First, quality and safety incidents require a parallel escalation to an ML engineer at Tier 1 — a domain that traditional SRE escalation chains do not include. Second, safety incidents bypass the usual time-based escalation and go directly to executive notification because the reputational and regulatory exposure of a safety incident cannot wait for a 60-minute resolution window.

attrs.alt: Four-tier escalation chain: Tier 1 on-call engineers, Tier 2 management awareness, Tier 3 executive escalation, Tier 4 C-level for safety incidents

content.text: The Four Escalation Tiers
Tier
Who
When Activated
Method
Tier 1
SRE on-call primary; ML secondary (quality/safety); Platform on-call (infra)
Immediately at alert time — auto-paged by PagerDuty
PagerDuty page
Tier 2
Engineering Manager; Feature Tech Lead / Staff Engineer
P1 at T+15 min (notify); P1 at T+30 min unresolved (page); P2 at T+30 min (notify)
Slack notify → PD page
Tier 3
VP Engineering; Product Director
P1 unresolved at T+60 min; safety incident confirmed after T+15
PagerDuty page or direct call
Tier 4
CTO / CEO
Safety incident confirmed OR P1 > 2h unresolved OR budget exhausted with SLA impact
Direct call

The distinction between 'notify' and 'page' matters. A Slack notification reaches the EM asynchronously — they will see it when they check their phone. A PagerDuty page wakes them up regardless. Use notify for awareness; use page only when their active involvement is required. Paging the EM at T+15 for a P1 that resolves at T+20 produces alert fatigue and erodes the page's signal.
Time-Based Paging Policy Implementation

Automate the time-based escalation logic so that the on-call engineer does not need to manually trigger escalations. The incident lifecycle manager calls get_paging_actions() at regular intervals and dispatches any new actions.

# paging_policy.py — determines who to page given severity and elapsed time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Severity(Enum):
    P1 = 1
    P2 = 2
    P3 = 3
    P4 = 4

@dataclass
class PagingAction:
    schedule_id: str
    method: str      # 'page' (PagerDuty) or 'notify' (Slack)
    message: str

def get_paging_actions(
    severity: Severity,
    minutes_elapsed: float,
    safety_violation: bool = False,
    eval_quality_incident: bool = False,
    still_unresolved: bool = True,
) -> list[PagingAction]:
    actions: list[PagingAction] = []

    # Tier 1: always paged at incident open
    if minutes_elapsed == 0:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='sre-primary-schedule',
            method='page',
            message='Production incident detected — SRE primary on-call',
        ))
        # Safety and eval quality also auto-page ML secondary
        if safety_violation or eval_quality_incident:
            actions.append(PagingAction(
                schedule_id='ml-secondary-schedule',
                method='page',
                message='Quality/safety incident — ML secondary on-call',
            ))
        return actions

    if not still_unresolved:
        return []  # Resolved — no more paging

    # Tier 2: EM notified at T+15 for P1, T+30 for P2
    if severity == Severity.P1 and minutes_elapsed >= 15:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='engineering-manager',
            method='notify',
            message='[P1 ACTIVE] Engineering manager awareness notification',
        ))
    if severity == Severity.P2 and minutes_elapsed >= 30:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='engineering-manager',
            method='notify',
            message='[P2 ACTIVE 30min] Engineering manager notification',
        ))

    # Tier 2: EM paged (not just notified) at T+30 for P1
    if severity == Severity.P1 and minutes_elapsed >= 30:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='engineering-manager',
            method='page',
            message='[P1 30MIN UNRESOLVED] Escalating to Engineering Manager',
        ))

    # Tier 3: VP Eng paged at T+60 for unresolved P1
    if severity == Severity.P1 and minutes_elapsed >= 60:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='vp-engineering',
            method='page',
            message='[P1 60MIN UNRESOLVED] Escalating to VP Engineering',
        ))

    # Tier 4: C-level for safety incidents confirmed after T+15
    if safety_violation and minutes_elapsed >= 15:
        actions.append(PagingAction(
            schedule_id='cto-oncall',
            method='page',
            message='[SAFETY INCIDENT] C-level notification required',
        ))

    return actions

The paging policy function is pure — it takes the current state and elapsed time and returns what actions should be taken. It does not dispatch anything itself. This makes it testable: you can assert that a safety_violation=True incident returns a C-level page at T+15 without needing to actually fire a PagerDuty event.
Contact Registry: One Source of Truth

A common failure mode in escalation chains is contact information stored in runbooks that go stale. When the EM changes, ten different runbooks need to be updated. A central contact registry loaded from environment variables or a secrets manager ensures that a single update propagates everywhere.

# contact_registry.py — central contact store for escalation chain
# Store as environment config; rotate contact info here, not in runbooks.
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Contact:
    name: str
    pagerduty_user_id: str
    slack_user_id: str
    phone: str            # for direct call as last resort
    role: str
    escalation_tier: int  # 1, 2, 3, or 4

# Contacts are loaded from environment / secrets manager at runtime.
# This avoids hard-coding names and numbers in runbooks that drift.
def load_escalation_contacts() -> dict[str, Contact]:
    return {
        'sre_oncall': Contact(
            name=os.environ.get('SRE_ONCALL_NAME', 'SRE On-Call'),
            pagerduty_user_id=os.environ['SRE_PD_USER_ID'],
            slack_user_id=os.environ['SRE_SLACK_USER_ID'],
            phone=os.environ['SRE_ONCALL_PHONE'],
            role='SRE Primary On-Call',
            escalation_tier=1,
        ),
        'ml_oncall': Contact(
            name=os.environ.get('ML_ONCALL_NAME', 'ML On-Call'),
            pagerduty_user_id=os.environ['ML_PD_USER_ID'],
            slack_user_id=os.environ['ML_SLACK_USER_ID'],
            phone=os.environ['ML_ONCALL_PHONE'],
            role='ML Secondary On-Call',
            escalation_tier=1,
        ),
        'engineering_manager': Contact(
            name=os.environ['EM_NAME'],
            pagerduty_user_id=os.environ['EM_PD_USER_ID'],
            slack_user_id=os.environ['EM_SLACK_USER_ID'],
            phone=os.environ['EM_PHONE'],
            role='Engineering Manager',
            escalation_tier=2,
        ),
        'vp_engineering': Contact(
            name=os.environ['VP_ENG_NAME'],
            pagerduty_user_id=os.environ['VP_ENG_PD_USER_ID'],
            slack_user_id=os.environ['VP_ENG_SLACK_USER_ID'],
            phone=os.environ['VP_ENG_PHONE'],
            role='VP Engineering',
            escalation_tier=3,
        ),
        'cto': Contact(
            name=os.environ['CTO_NAME'],
            pagerduty_user_id=os.environ['CTO_PD_USER_ID'],
            slack_user_id=os.environ['CTO_SLACK_USER_ID'],
            phone=os.environ['CTO_PHONE'],
            role='CTO',
            escalation_tier=4,
        ),
    }

def get_contact(role_key: str) -> Contact:
    contacts = load_escalation_contacts()
    if role_key not in contacts:
        raise KeyError(f'Unknown escalation contact: {role_key}')
    return contacts[role_key]

Store the registry values in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, etc.) and rotate them there when contacts change. Runbooks reference the registry by role key, not by name — 'engineering_manager' rather than 'Priya Sharma' — so that the runbook remains valid through personnel changes.
Provider Escalation: The Third Escalation Path

For GenAI services, there is a third escalation path that traditional SRE programs do not have: escalating to the LLM provider when the root cause is a provider incident. This is distinct from internal escalation — it involves a commercial relationship, TAM contacts, and provider support portals.
Provider Incident Type
Internal Action
Provider Escalation Action
API availability outage
Activate fallback model or queue requests
Check status page; open P1 support ticket if not acknowledged by provider
Latency spike (not our traffic pattern)
Route to secondary provider if available
Notify TAM; request acknowledgment on status page
Quality regression (model behavior changed)
Pin to last-known-good model version
Notify TAM; request rollback or confirmation of intentional change
Safety regression (model behavior changed)
Disable feature flag immediately; pin model version
Urgent TAM escalation; document for regulatory records

Always check the provider status page before creating a support ticket. If the provider has already acknowledged the incident on their status page, creating a ticket does not accelerate resolution — it just adds noise. Create the ticket only if the incident is not acknowledged after 15 minutes or if you need an SLA-backed resolution commitment.
Escalation Anti-Patterns
Skipping tiers: paging the VP at T+5 for a P1 that is actively being worked removes the EM from the loop and creates executive alert fatigue
Under-escalating safety incidents: treating a safety violation as a P2 to avoid waking leadership is the wrong trade-off — safety incidents are always P1 with C-level notification
Name-based contact lists in runbooks: 'call Priya' breaks the moment Priya leaves the team; role-based registry is mandatory
No escalation for prolonged P2: a P2 incident that persists for 6 hours without escalation is functionally a P1 — enforce a T+4h EM escalation for any unresolved P2

Assuming provider acknowledgment means resolution: a status page update from the provider says they are aware of the issue, not that it is fixed — continue internal mitigation (fallback model, circuit breaker) in parallel
Testing the Escalation Chain

Run a quarterly fire drill that simulates a P1 incident and exercises the full escalation chain without a real production impact. The drill verifies that PagerDuty schedules are correct, contact information is current, and every person in the chain knows what is expected of them when they receive a page.
Announce the drill date to all participants; do not run surprise drills during business hours
Trigger a synthetic P1 alert in PagerDuty pointing at the drill runbook
Verify that Tier 1 pages arrive within the ack SLA (15 min)
At T+15, verify that Tier 2 Slack notification fires correctly
At T+30, verify that Tier 2 page fires correctly
At T+60, verify Tier 3 page logic (can be simulated without actually paging the VP)
Review drill results in the weekly SRE retro; fix any gaps before the next rotation
Summary

A tiered escalation chain for GenAI services has four levels: Tier 1 on-call engineers (SRE primary, ML secondary for quality/safety, Platform for infra), Tier 2 management awareness at time thresholds, Tier 3 executive involvement for prolonged or major incidents, and Tier 4 C-level for confirmed safety incidents or budget-exhaustion events. Automate time-based paging with a pure paging policy function that is testable independently of the dispatch mechanism. Centralize contact information in a role-keyed registry to prevent stale runbooks. Add LLM provider escalation as a third escalation path alongside internal tiers. Test the chain with quarterly drills to verify that schedules, contacts, and knowledge are current before a real incident requires them.
